attributes.stem: A previously well 31-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a six hour history of severe colicky right loin pain radiating down into his groin. He has no other symptoms. On examination his blood pressure is 124/76 mmHg, pulse 96/min and temperature 37.2oC. His abdomen is soft, apart from some mild tenderness in the right flank. Urinalysis shows blood +++. A non-contrast CT scan shows an 8mm stone in the upper right ureter, with mild hydronephrosis and some right-sided perinephric fat stranding.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be most suggestive that he should be managed in hospital?
options: Pain radiating down to the groin
The size and site of the stone
attributes.explanation:
Many patients with ureterolithiasis (renal stone in the ureter) can be successfully managed with analgesia while the stone passes on its own (conservative management). There are indications for intervention in the management of a patient with renal colic and these include:

  • Signs and symptoms of infection

  • Inability to control pain despite maximum analgesia

  • Certain professions such as airline pilots

  • A stone in a solitary or transplanted kidney

  • Evidence of significant renal impairment from baseline

  • Prolonged unrelieved obstruction

This patient has a sizeable proximal stone that is causing symptoms. The likelihood of spontaneous passage of a stone this size is low (less than 9%). The stone may cause obstruction to flow with resultant hydronephrosis. Whilst such obstruction could take several weeks to cause long-term renal impairment, stones of this size are unlikely to pass spontaneously over this time and thus can be predicted to have prolonged, unrelieved obstruction unless intervention is performed. This would either be in the form of a primary ureteroscopy and laser lithotripsy or a cystoscopy and right ureteric stent insertion with a plan to return to treat the stone at a second procedure if the ureter was unable to accessed because it was too tight or there were other technical issues.

The presence of blood in the urine in this case means no more than a stone being present in the urinary tract. The site and distribution of the pain is not an indication of either severity of the problem, nor any need for hospitalisation. Likewise, the mild tenderness in itself is not an indication for admitting the patient. However, any tenderness must raise awareness of possible sepsis, however in this case there is little other clinical evidence of this. Perinephric stranding can be indicative of an inflammatory process, but in most cases, such as the one described in the stem, it is related to the mild degree of hydronephrosis.
learningPoints: Stones greater than 7 mm are unlikely to pass down the ureter spontaneously.
references: Arumuham V, Byroft J. The Management of Urolithiasis. Surgery. 2016;34(7):352-360
Jendeberg J, Geijer H, Alshamari M, et al. Size matters: The width and location of a ureteral stone accurately predict the chance of spontaneous passage. Eur Radiol 2017; 27:4775-85.

Fukami H, Takeuchi Y, Kagaya S, et a. Perirenal fat stranding is not a powerful diagnostic
tool for acute pyelonephritis. Int J Gen Med 2017:10 137–44.
